records.ety: [OE. swounen, swoghenen, for swo<?/nien, fr. swo<?/en to sigh deeply, to droop, AS. swōgan to sough, sigh; cf. geswōgen senseless, swooned, geswōwung a swooning. Cf. Sough.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Swooned (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Swooning.]

defs: To sink into a fainting fit, in which there is an apparent suspension of the vital functions and mental powers; to faint; -- often with away.
quotes: The sucklings swoon in the streets of the city.
The most in years . . . swooned first away for pain.
He seemed ready to swoon away in the surprise of joy.
